recipes.creator: caseyneistat
recipes.hook: I don't know what I'm talking about
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: cryptic/warning-style posts function as teasers when the creator already has audience trust. Casey's massive base means viewers will watch a cryptic post just to figure out what's happening. Low-friction engagement.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Poor fit. Cryptic posts require existing audience scale to land. Dan's audience is too small for this format to work — would just read as confused.

recipe.name: Cryptic teaser (audience-scale-dependent)
recipe.concept: An intentionally opaque post with no voice-over and a single emoji caption. Works only when the audience already trusts the creator enough to investigate.
ingredients: existing audience that will engage with mystery
a song bed with an evocative line
a one-emoji caption
ambiguous visuals
a real reveal coming in a follow-up post
steps: [Anti-pattern at Dan's scale. Document for recognition only.]
recipe.adaptation_notes: Revisit this format once Dan's audience is large enough that mystery is a feature, not noise. Probably 50K+ followers.
recipe.dan_version: Don't run yet. Note the format for later — useful when audience scale changes the economics of attention.

dan_versions.design: Don't run yet. Note the format for later — useful when audience scale changes the economics of attention.

recipe.what_happens: Cryptic post — emoji-only caption (⚠️) with a song sample that loops "I don't know what I'm talking about." 47.9M views suggests it's either a viral moment or this transcript is incomplete. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: cryptic/warning-style posts function as teasers when the creator already has audience trust. Casey's massive base means viewers will watch a cryptic post just to figure out what's happening. Low-friction engagement.
recipe.title: @caseyneistat — Cryptic teaser (audience-scale-dependent)

recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: ad-disguised-as-tutorial is one of the most lucrative TikTok formats. The DIY hook (cherry blossom wreath) earns the watch, the product placement is integrated, the budget framing ("on a budget") is the audience match. Works because the tutorial is genuinely usable even without the product.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Anti-pattern for Dan per `avoid: performative hustle, generic AI bro content`. Sponsored content might be appropriate eventually but the format here — tutorial-as-sales-vehicle — drifts into the territory Dan's config rejects.

recipe.name: Ad-disguised-as-tutorial (ANTI-PATTERN unless paid + transparent)
recipe.concept: A how-to post built around a sponsored product, with product mentions woven into the tutorial steps. The audience gets a real tutorial; the brand gets seamless placement.
ingredients: a sponsor with an actual product
a genuine DIY tutorial
product placement at 2-3 specific moments
a deal mention (price, deadline)
a clear partnership disclosure
steps: [Anti-pattern unless paid, disclosed, and aligned with the audience.]
recipe.adaptation_notes: If Dan ever does sponsored content for Robson Studio, the disclosure has to be unmissable, the product has to be one Dan actually uses, and the format should be honest about being an ad — not disguised as a tutorial.
recipe.dan_version: Don't run as anti-pattern. If a real sponsorship appears, structure it as "how I actually use [tool] in real work" — never as a tutorial-with-product-baked-in.

dan_versions.design: Don't run as anti-pattern. If a real sponsorship appears, structure it as "how I actually use [tool] in real work" — never as a tutorial-with-product-baked-in.

recipe.what_happens: Sponsored Febreze ad disguised as a DIY craft post. Cherry blossom wreath tutorial integrated with Febreze plug scent booster placement ("available in black"). Includes price ($5 off) and a deadline (until January 24). Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: ad-disguised-as-tutorial is one of the most lucrative TikTok formats. The DIY hook (cherry blossom wreath) earns the watch, the product placement is integrated, the budget framing ("on a budget") is the audience match. Works because the tutorial is genuinely usable even without the product.

recipes.creator: caseyneistat
recipes.caption: i wear everyday.  it attracts a certain kind of lady.
recipes.hook: Cologne smells like WD-40. This is actual men's cologne that's meant to smell like WD-40.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: the hook is the entire post. "Cologne smells like WD-40" is so absurd it earns a 5-second watch. The test is the proof. The two-word verdict closes it. Total runtime probably under 15 seconds. No padding.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Decent fit for short curiosity content. The pattern — "absurd product/tool/feature exists, I tested it, here's the verdict" — works for novelty AI tools, weird Figma plugins, niche design products. Keep it dry — Casey's verdict is funny because it's deadpan.

recipe.name: Absurd-product try-it
recipe.concept: Open with an absurd claim about a real product, run the test on camera, deliver a deadpan verdict in fewer than 10 words.
ingredients: a real but absurd product
the product visible in-frame
one clear test
a two-line verdict
total runtime under 15 seconds
steps: State the absurdity flat: "This is [thing] that does [absurd thing]."
Show the product clearly.
Do the test in one shot.
Deliver the verdict deadpan.
Cut. No outro.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Dry verdict is the work. Don't soften it with a smile.
recipe.dan_version: "This is a Figma plugin that promises to design your entire UI in one prompt. I'm going to try it." Show the plugin. Run the prompt. Cut to the result. Verdict: "As bad as you'd imagine. Worse, actually."

dan_versions.design: "This is a Figma plugin that promises to design your entire UI in one prompt. I'm going to try it." Show the plugin. Run the prompt. Cut to the result. Verdict: "As bad as you'd imagine. Worse, actually."

recipe.what_happens: Curiosity-driven product try-it. Opens with the absurd claim (cologne that smells like WD-40), names that it's a real product, runs the test (smells it), delivers the verdict in two words: "it's as awful as you'd imagine." Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: the hook is the entire post. "Cologne smells like WD-40" is so absurd it earns a 5-second watch. The test is the proof. The two-word verdict closes it. Total runtime probably under 15 seconds. No padding.
recipe.title: @caseyneistat — Absurd-product try-it

recipe.name: Craft-reveal with labor-cost caption

ingredients: a finished piece of craft work
one spoken reaction line
a specific labor-cost number in the caption
an optional tutorial offer
high-quality footage of the work
steps: Open on the finished piece.
One reaction line.
Caption: hours/days it took.
Optional: tutorial CTA.
Don't explain the technique in the video.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Labor cost must be real. The format trades on honesty about how long things actually take.

recipes.creator: markrober
recipes.caption: pride comes before flop ft. @lacy @marlon3lg @ksi @mrbeast @chunkz @t...
recipes.hook: How many goals do you score tonight?
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: celebrity collab + setup-pay-off structure is a near-guaranteed pattern at scale. Each prediction is a tiny narrative bet the audience holds in their head, then resolves in the second half. Works because the creators are recognisable and the stakes are low.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Poor direct fit (no creator network of that scale). The transferable element is the setup-payoff structure — "I asked everyone what they expect, here's what actually happened." Works for small-scale: clients predicting which design will win, designers predicting which AI tool will be best.

recipe.name: Pre-event prediction interviews → reveal
recipe.concept: Interview multiple people predicting a specific outcome before an event. Resolve all predictions afterwards. The structure earns the watch because each prediction is a held bet.
ingredients: 3+ people willing to go on record
a specific predictable outcome
a real event coming
a payoff video resolving each prediction
named people, not anonymous
steps: Find an upcoming event with a measurable outcome.
Get 3-5 specific predictions on camera.
Make each prediction specific (a number, a name).
Run the event.
Cut a payoff video resolving each prediction.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Scale is the constraint. Dan can do this with 3 designer friends predicting which of 3 design directions wins a client vote, or 3 founders predicting which AI tool ships fastest.
recipe.dan_version: "I sent the same brief to four designers and asked them to predict which approach the client would pick." Show four predictions. Then the client decision. Then the reveal — who was right and why.

dan_versions.design: "I sent the same brief to four designers and asked them to predict which approach the client would pick." Show four predictions. Then the client decision. Then the reveal — who was right and why.

recipe.what_happens: Pre-event prediction interviews with named creators (KSI, MrBeast, Chunkz, etc.) before a soccer charity match. Each predicts their goal count. The post implies a payoff (the actual match) but ends on the chaos of the game itself. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: celebrity collab + setup-pay-off structure is a near-guaranteed pattern at scale. Each prediction is a tiny narrative bet the audience holds in their head, then resolves in the second half. Works because the creators are recognisable and the stakes are low.
recipe.title: @markrober — Pre-event prediction interviews → reveal

recipes.creator: markrober
recipes.caption: Testing If You Can Breathe Underwater!
recipes.hook: Can you really breathe with a bamboo reed underwater like they do in the cartoons?
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: "can you really X like in the cartoons" is a strong hook because it taps childhood curiosity. The structure — test, fail, explain with a visual analogy, conclude — is canonical popular science. The water bottle as lung analogy is the memorable detail.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Good structural fit. The pattern — "can you really X like the marketing claims?" — translates directly to AI tool claims. Run the test, fail or succeed clearly, explain why with a visual analogy, conclude.

recipe.name: Test-the-claim with visual analogy
recipe.concept: Take a popular claim ("you can X"), test it on camera at multiple thresholds, explain the underlying reason with a visible analogy, conclude with a specific threshold answer.
ingredients: a widely-believed claim
2-3 test thresholds
a visible analogy explaining the physics/logic
a clear pass/fail per threshold
a precise concluding answer
steps: State the claim as a question.
Test threshold 1. Show result.
Test threshold 2 (harder). Show result.
Explain the underlying reason with a visible analogy.
Test threshold 3 if it adds info. State the precise answer.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Visual analogy is the work. The water bottle as lung is what makes this post sticky.
recipe.dan_version: "Can you really build a full app in one prompt like the demos show?" Test 1: "Build me a todo app." Pass. Test 2: "Build me a Figma plugin." Soft fail. Analogy: "A prompt is like a brief — the more context, the better the output." Test 3: "Build me a multi-page SaaS dashboard." Hard fail. Conclude: "Yes for utilities. No past three screens."

dan_versions.design: "Can you really build a full app in one prompt like the demos show?" Test 1: "Build me a todo app." Pass. Test 2: "Build me a Figma plugin." Soft fail. Analogy: "A prompt is like a brief — the more context, the better the output." Test 3: "Build me a multi-page SaaS dashboard." Hard fail. Conclude: "Yes for utilities. No past three screens."

recipe.what_happens: Test-the-myth educational post. Tests three bamboo reed lengths (1ft, 2ft, 3ft). Explains why with a water bottle analogy (lung being crushed by water pressure). Concludes: yes at shallow depths, no past 2 feet. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: "can you really X like in the cartoons" is a strong hook because it taps childhood curiosity. The structure — test, fail, explain with a visual analogy, conclude — is canonical popular science. The water bottle as lung analogy is the memorable detail.
recipe.title: @markrober — Test-the-claim with visual analogy

recipes.hook: As you start your animation journey, the initial goal is to make a drop go down.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: structured skill-ladder content (beginner → intermediate → advanced) is high-utility. The 'save this for later' CTA is the engagement hook. Visual demonstrations of each level prove it's real. 5.9M views shows skill-content scale.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit. The pattern — 3-level skill ladder on a specific topic — translates directly to design, AI, brand work. 'Initial goal: get the AI to write one line. Then: get it to match your voice. Then: get it to write the next post for you.'

recipe.name: Three-level skill ladder
recipe.concept: Walk a single skill from beginner to advanced in 3 explicit levels. Show each level visually. Save-this-post CTA at the end.
ingredients: a single specific skill
3 explicit difficulty levels
a visual demonstration per level
a save-this CTA
no jargon
steps: Name the skill in plain language.
Level 1: the absolute basic.
Level 2: one specific advancement.
Level 3: integration into a real use case.
End with 'save this for later'.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Levels must be honest. Skipping levels reads as gatekeeping.
recipe.dan_version: 'Using AI for design work, three levels. Level 1: ask Claude for component variants. Level 2: feed it your brand tokens and constraints. Level 3: build a Skill that knows your typography rules and audit any frame against them in one prompt.' Show each level. Save this.

dan_versions.design: 'Using AI for design work, three levels. Level 1: ask Claude for component variants. Level 2: feed it your brand tokens and constraints. Level 3: build a Skill that knows your typography rules and audit any frame against them in one prompt.' Show each level. Save this.

recipe.what_happens: Animation tutorial micro-essay. Walks 3 levels of skill: drop going down → acceleration → place in a scene with stretch + highlights. Save-this-post CTA at end. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: structured skill-ladder content (beginner → intermediate → advanced) is high-utility. The 'save this for later' CTA is the engagement hook. Visual demonstrations of each level prove it's real. 5.9M views shows skill-content scale.

recipes.hook: Why am I cutting my husband's text messages from 13 years ago?

recipes.fit_for_dan: Moderate fit, with translation. The pattern — "why am I [unusual specific action]?" → "because I'm turning [personal archive] into [physical thing]" — works if Dan has an archive worth transforming. Project archives, sketchbook archives, the Apple Notes export.

ingredients: a personal archive (notes, messages, sketches, photos)
an unusual physical transformation
a hook question naming the unusual action
a specific number (years, items)
the transformation visible in steps
steps: Open with the question — make the action sound strange.
Answer it with the project.
Name the archive's scale (13 years, 600 messages, 4 sketchbooks).
Show the transformation steps.
End on the finished piece.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Archive has to be real. The format collapses if the archive is fake or hastily created.
recipe.dan_version: "Why am I printing every sketch I made in 2024?" Because I'm turning the year of work-on-paper into a single physical book for my desk. Show the count. Show the print run. Show the binding. Close on the book on the shelf.

recipes.creator: markrober
recipes.caption: How Record Players Actually Work!
recipes.hook: This is the world's most simple record player in recorders.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: build-from-scratch demonstrations of how a familiar thing works are extremely sticky. The audience thinks they know what a record player does, but seeing the actual physics rebuilt from foil makes the concept undeniable. The aluminum foil moment is the memorable detail.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit for AI/design explainers. The pattern — "here's how [familiar thing] actually works, built from scratch" — works for prompt engineering, design tokens, brand systems, type rendering. Build-the-minimal-version is the work.

recipe.name: Build-from-scratch first-principles explainer
recipe.concept: Take a familiar thing the audience uses but doesn't understand. Build the simplest possible version on camera. Use the simple version to prove the principle.
ingredients: a familiar tool or system
the principle it operates on
the minimal materials to recreate the principle
a working demonstration
a memorable visual moment
steps: Name the thing the audience uses every day.
State the principle in one sentence.
Build a minimal physical version.
Demonstrate the principle works.
Close on the working minimal version.
recipe.adaptation_notes: The minimal version has to actually work. Don't fake it.
recipe.dan_version: "This is the world's simplest design token system." Build it from one Sheets file. Show how a brand uses it: when the cell changes, every screen updates. Close on the working version. Then: "That's also how every real design system works. Just with more cells."

dan_versions.design: "This is the world's simplest design token system." Build it from one Sheets file. Show how a brand uses it: when the cell changes, every screen updates. Close on the working version. Then: "That's also how every real design system works. Just with more cells."

recipe.what_happens: First-principles educational explainer. Shows how a record player works by building one from scratch. Demonstrates the principle: needle vibrates in groove → vibrates in same way as the original sound. Then proves it by carving voice vibrations into spinning aluminum foil and playing it back. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: build-from-scratch demonstrations of how a familiar thing works are extremely sticky. The audience thinks they know what a record player does, but seeing the actual physics rebuilt from foil makes the concept undeniable. The aluminum foil moment is the memorable detail.
recipe.title: @markrober — Build-from-scratch first-principles explainer

recipes.creator: caseyneistat
recipes.caption: it’s just a proposal for now but i worry if the city makes snitching ...
recipes.hook: How to make seventy two thousand eight hundred dollars a year snitching on bike lane violations.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: "how to make $X doing Y" is a heavy hook. The on-screen math + real footage of finding 8 violations in 3 hours is the proof. Mostly travels because it surfaces an obscure city bill in a way that's both useful and absurd.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Moderate fit. The pattern — "here's the actual money math on this obscure thing" — translates to AI tooling ("here's the actual cost of running Claude for a real design project"), or design industry ("here's what a SaaS brand refresh actually costs hour-by-hour").

recipe.name: Math-the-loophole breakdown
recipe.concept: Find a real economic loophole or obscure mechanism. Show the math on camera. The math is the post.
ingredients: a real obscure mechanism (bill, policy, price quirk)
specific verifiable numbers
on-camera or on-screen calculation
real footage as proof of feasibility
a final annual or weekly figure
steps: Hook: "How to make/save $X doing Y."
Explain the mechanism in two sentences.
Show one real instance in the field.
Multiply through to the headline number.
End on the number, not commentary.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Has to be real. Faking the math kills credibility instantly.
recipe.dan_version: "How to save $14,000 a year on your SaaS stack by switching three tools." Show the receipts. Stack math: $X Figma + $Y Notion + $Z others. Compare to the cheaper stack. End on the annual difference.

dan_versions.design: "How to save $14,000 a year on your SaaS stack by switching three tools." Show the receipts. Stack math: $X Figma + $Y Notion + $Z others. Compare to the cheaper stack. End on the annual difference.

recipe.what_happens: Investigative how-much-could-you-make breakdown of a proposed NYC bike lane bounty bill. Walks the actual math: 8 violations × $175 × 25% bounty × 4 days = $1,400/wk = ~$72,800/yr. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: "how to make $X doing Y" is a heavy hook. The on-screen math + real footage of finding 8 violations in 3 hours is the proof. Mostly travels because it surfaces an obscure city bill in a way that's both useful and absurd.
recipe.title: @caseyneistat — Math-the-loophole breakdown

recipe.name: Diagnose-then-give playful template offer

a mock-serious diagnosis line
a specific remixable template/file
a clear use case demonstrated
a frictionless way to get the file

Pivot to the fix.
Show the template in use.
Tell them where to get it.

recipe.name: Anti-advice creator-economy honesty
recipe.concept: Take a piece of widely-given advice. Name the emotional texture of what actually happens when you follow it. The honesty is the post.
ingredients: a widely-given piece of advice
the actual unspoken experience of following it
one shame or doubt moment named specifically
no resolution or fix
a first-person voice
steps: Open with the standard advice.
Pivot to what actually happens.
Name the shame or doubt moment specifically.
Don't fix it.
Close on the honest position.

recipe.dan_version: 'Everyone says use AI to ship faster. What no one says is how it feels to spend a month polishing the AI output to the point where you could have just done it yourself in three days. Or that the speed you gain isn't time saved, it's just more variations to choose between. Or that picking the right one is now the whole job and nobody respects selection like they respect creation.'

recipe.name: Unreasonable-on-purpose domestic gag

steps: Open with the small action mid-stream (no preamble).

Cut on the punchline — no resolution needed.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Dan's version: solo voice, not partner banter. The skeptical voice can be inner monologue or a friend's text on screen. Specificity is the work — name the actual decision ("I'm switching the studio's primary typeface and we don't have a brief yet") rather than gesturing at one.

recipe.name: Friend/client-house transformation with running characterisation

a real second person who appears
a running characterisation ("indecisive queen")
a specific design decision per beat
the second person's reaction at the end
steps: Open with the second person and the space.
Establish the running characterisation early.
Make each design beat reference the person's traits.
Show 3-4 specific decisions.
End on the reaction.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Requires the second person's consent and comfort being characterised on camera.

an era-appropriate song
a 2-word caption naming the feeling
no voice-over
audience-shared cultural memory
steps: Pick a specific nostalgic visual (an old interface, a 90s aesthetic, an early-internet artifact).
Match an era-appropriate song.
Caption: 'X unlocked' or similar.
No voice-over.
Let recognition do the work.

dan_versions.design: Skeuomorphic iOS 6 buttons. Aqua mac OS X. Web 2.0 gloss. Real design nostalgia for the era Dan was actually working through. 'iOS skeuomorphism unlocked' as caption. Era-appropriate song. No voice-over.

recipes.creator: clare.sullivan_
recipes.caption: Pls if you tell me I should’ve sanded……. I KNOWWWWWWWW but even me, u...
recipes.hook: I hate when you guys are right and I am wrong, but my floor does look like shit.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: admitting commenters were right + showing the fix in progress is engagement gold. Audience that left advice feels validated. Audience that watches feels invested in the fix. Self-deprecation defuses the 'I was wrong' beat.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit. The pattern — 'commenters were right, here's the fix' — is honest, audience-respecting, and works in Dan's voice. Run when a design choice gets pushback that actually lands.

recipe.name: Commenters-were-right correction post
recipe.concept: Acknowledge that the comments on a prior post were correct. Show the fix in progress. Don't grovel; treat the correction as material for the next post.
ingredients: a prior post that got pushback
an honest 'they were right' acknowledgement
the fix in progress
no over-apology
a continuation of the project
steps: Open with the acknowledgement.
Show the issue.
Walk the fix.
Don't grovel.
Close on the in-progress state.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Only run when the commenters were genuinely right. Performed humility reads worse than ignoring criticism.
recipe.dan_version: 'Comments on the Whiting site said the hero section was too quiet. Looking at it again, they're right. Here's what I'm trying instead.' Show three new directions. Don't apologise.

dan_versions.design: 'Comments on the Whiting site said the hero section was too quiet. Looking at it again, they're right. Here's what I'm trying instead.' Show three new directions. Don't apologise.

recipe.what_happens: Self-deprecating mistake post. Acknowledges commenters were right (should have sanded floor first). Sets up the fix: testing paint mixes like a chemist. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: admitting commenters were right + showing the fix in progress is engagement gold. Audience that left advice feels validated. Audience that watches feels invested in the fix. Self-deprecation defuses the 'I was wrong' beat.

recipes.fit_for_dan: EXCELLENT fit. Brand critique with a specific technical position is exactly Dan's lane. The diplomatic opener pattern keeps it from reading as a takedown.

recipe.name: Diplomatic brand critique with technical reasoning
recipe.concept: Critique a recognisable brand decision. Open diplomatically (I love X but...). Walk 2-3 specific technical issues. Land on the core problem.
ingredients: a recognisable brand decision
a diplomatic opener
2-3 specific technical issues
a core problem statement
no schadenfreude
steps: Open diplomatically.
Show the application examples.
Name 2-3 specific issues.
Build to the core problem.
Close on the principle, not the brand.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Diplomatic opener is the safety mechanism — protects from looking like a hater. Specificity is the credibility.
recipe.dan_version: 'I love Linear's product but the new pricing page rubs me the wrong way. The hierarchy on the plans card is confused — the most expensive plan is visually equal to the cheapest. The cognitive load to compare is higher than it should be. The core problem: they're pricing by team size but selling by feature.'

dan_versions.design: 'I love Linear's product but the new pricing page rubs me the wrong way. The hierarchy on the plans card is confused — the most expensive plan is visually equal to the cheapest. recipes.creator: renovationhusbands
recipes.caption: It’s been almost a year since we completed our Boston attic renovatio...
recipes.hook: It's been almost a year since we completed our Boston attic renovation
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: time-lapse / anniversary framing performs well because it sells the audience two things at once — the original transformation and the lived-in result. The implication "we're still happy with it" is the actual hook for renovation audiences.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Good structural fit. Dan ships projects (Whiting, King Living, OpenScripture, robson.studio). A "year after we launched" tour-back works for design work, especially when the brief, the result, and how it aged are visible side by side.
recipes.risks: Transcript may contain ASR errors. Visual/context claims still require manual review.

recipe.name: Anniversary tour-back
recipe.concept: Return to a finished project at a clean time interval and show what aged, what held, what you'd change.
ingredients: a specific time interval (1 year, 6 months, 90 days)
the original brief or before-state in one frame
the current state in the second frame
one honest line of self-critique
steps: State the project and interval in the caption, not the hook.
Open on the current state, not the before.
Cut to the before — a screenshot, sketch, or photo.
Walk one feature: what held, what surprised you, what you'd change.
Close on the current state with one quiet line of judgement, not a recap.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Voice-over here is a song bed — the work is in the caption + visuals. Dan's voice-driven version should keep the same restraint: one honest self-critique line is worth more than three boasts.
recipe.dan_version: "It's been a year since robson.studio went live. What I'd change: the hero section. What aged well: the no-testimonials choice."

dan_versions.design: "It's been a year since robson.studio went live. What I'd change: the hero section. What aged well: the no-testimonials choice."

recipes.creator: mattymatheson
recipes.caption: AM I LOSING SIGHT BECAUSE OF RAMEN PARASITES?!
recipes.hook: My kids eat instant ramen multiple times a week. I am not about to listen to you commenters tell me about the instant ramen parasites.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: directly addressing recurring comments turns a chronic distraction into a payable post. The microscope is a comedic prop. The dismissal ("I don't believe in that") frames Matty as anti-panic.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Moderate fit. The pattern — "comments keep saying X, here's me actually testing X" — works for AI design panic content ("comments keep saying AI will replace designers; here's me actually trying to ship a brand with only Claude").

recipe.name: Confront-the-recurring-comment
recipe.concept: Name a comment topic that keeps coming up. Actually test it on camera. Land the dismissal or confirmation based on what you find.
ingredients: a recurring comment topic
a way to test the claim physically
honest reaction to the test
a final position based on what you saw
no smug delivery
steps: State the recurring comment in one sentence.
Bring out the test prop.
Run the test on camera.
Show whether the claim holds.
State your position based on the test, not on the comments.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Don't run unless you'll actually test it. Performative skepticism without a real test is worse than ignoring the comments.
recipe.dan_version: "Comments keep saying AI will design entire brand systems in 6 months. Today I'm going to actually try." Pull up Claude. Brief it. Show the output. State the position based on the result.

dan_versions.design: "Comments keep saying AI will design entire brand systems in 6 months. Today I'm going to actually try." Pull up Claude. Brief it. Show the output. State the position based on the result.

recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: directly addressing recurring comments turns a chronic distraction into a payable post. The microscope is a comedic prop. The dismissal ("I don't believe in that") frames Matty as anti-panic.
recipe.title: @mattymatheson — Confront-the-recurring-comment

Comedic because the "features" are basically a person being annoying, but framed flatteringly. Sharable for the recognition factor.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Solid structural fit for design content. The pattern translates directly to taking an annoying-thing-designers-do (over-kerning, sending Figma comments at 9pm, asking for the brief) and listing it as a value proposition.
recipes.risks: Transcript may contain ASR errors. Visual/context claims still require manual review.

recipe.name: Product-rebrand-as-identity
recipe.concept: Take a thing you're teased for, frame it as a list of premium product features, deliver in spec-sheet voice.
ingredients: the trait you're known for (or accused of)
3-4 specific behaviours that count as "features"
spec-sheet delivery cadence (no jokes in the lines themselves)
a soft pull-back at the end so it's affectionate, not boastful
steps: Open with "Of course I'm a [label]" — neutral, not defensive.
Walk through 3-4 features in plain product-spec voice.
Use the same sentence pattern each time (parallelism is the joke).
End on the trait that actually matters underneath the gag.
Cut without a punchline — the structure is the punchline.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Voice stays flat and confident. The humor is in the parallelism, not in punchlines. Dan should resist the urge to wink at the camera.

recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: stitch-reply rebuttals work because the original content's voice creates the setup, your voice creates the payoff. Mino opens by voicing the take he disagrees with so the audience feels the wrong, then redirects.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit. The pattern — voice the take you're rebutting, then ground your counter in personal experience — translates to AI/design discourse. 'Designers are dead because AI exists' is the take Dan could voice then counter from his actual work.

recipe.name: Stitch-rebuttal with personal counter
recipe.concept: Voice the take you disagree with in the original creator's voice. Then ground your rebuttal in specific personal experience, not abstract argument.
ingredients: a real take being made elsewhere
you voicing it accurately first
a personal experience that counters it
no name-calling
a calm pivot to the counter
steps: Open by voicing the original take faithfully.
Pause.
Counter with personal experience.
Don't generalise — stay specific.
Close on the principle.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Voice the take fairly. Strawmanning kills the format.
recipe.dan_version: Voice the take: 'AI is going to make designers obsolete.' Pause. Counter: 'I've used AI on every project this year. The actual job hasn't shrunk — it's shifted. The work used to be making 50 variations. Now it's choosing between 200. Selection is harder than generation. The designers who'll survive are the ones who learn taste, not the ones who avoid the tools.'

dan_versions.design: Voice the take: 'AI is going to make designers obsolete.' Pause. Counter: 'I've used AI on every project this year. The actual job hasn't shrunk — it's shifted. recipe.name: Period-piece pastiche
recipe.concept: Frame a current frustration inside a heightened earlier-era register (1950s commercial, 1980s news, etc.). The bit is the format itself.

a contemporary frustration as the actual content

no commentary breaking the frame
steps: Pick an era with strong audio/visual signature.
Write the script in that era's cadence — let the rhythm do the work.
Use real period-specific product names if you can.
Don't wink at the camera.
Land on the catchphrase, not a punchline.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Solo version risks feeling like a costume bit. Better when the era applies to actual work (a 50s-style poster for a real project, a 80s news graphic for a real launch).

Cut to the first practical step (always start working).
Establish one small gag or recurring beat early so it can return.

End on the finished state with one quiet line, not a brag.

recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: "two versions of a familiar object" hooks attention immediately. The structure — concrete object → historical why → physical differences → philosophical why — gives the viewer a complete frame. Drew teaches without lecturing because he hands the viewer specific evidence (weight, toe-box shape).
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit. Dan can do object-comparison content with design tools, brand systems, or AI workflows. The same structure works for: same Figma component in two systems, same prompt in Claude vs Cursor, same brand in 2018 vs 2026.
recipes.risks: Transcript may contain ASR errors. Visual/context claims still require manual review.

recipe.name: Comparative object reveal
recipe.concept: Hold up two versions of the same object that look identical, name a single visible difference, then unfold the historical and material reasons.
ingredients: two physical (or screen) versions of the same thing
a visible first difference shown in-frame
historical context that explains the split
2-3 specific material/structural differences
a closing line about philosophy, not just spec
steps: Open by pointing at a specific detail (a label, a corner, a line).
Reveal that the two are different.
Tell the historical reason — keep it tight.
Walk 2-3 specific differences with the objects in frame.
End on the philosophy or design value that produced the difference.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Dan's voice already runs in this register — specific, evidence-first, no hype. The risk is over-explaining. Keep the historical context to two sentences.
recipe.dan_version: "See this button? This is from a 2022 design system and this is from 2026." Show both. "In 2022 we shipped one variant per state. Now we ship six." Compare type sizes, corner radii, the actual token differences. End: "The philosophy shifted — accessibility isn't a variant, it's the default."

a hook line about the bewilderment
no resolution

List the evidence in 3-5 specific items.

No resolution.

recipes.creator: drewjoiner
recipes.caption: Why Kangols are worn backwards  #kangol #britishfashion #newyorkcityf...
recipes.hook: Kangles weren't meant to be worn like this, but that's what makes them so iconic.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: "why is this iconic thing the way it is" hooks immediately. The structure — anomaly → origin → name → cultural moment — gives the viewer a complete story arc in 60 seconds. Each beat earns the next. Acronym reveal ("K stands for silk") is a memorable detail viewers will repeat.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Excellent fit. The pattern works perfectly for design and tool history: why Helvetica is set this way, why Figma's autolayout works the way it does, why Tailwind looks like that. Dan can do these without being on-screen — captioned screen-recording works.

recipe.name: Etymology + cultural-arc explainer
recipe.concept: Frame an iconic thing's defining quirk as a question, then walk the history from origin → name → turning point → cultural adoption.
ingredients: an iconic object with a defining quirk
an origin story (year + person + place)
a name etymology or acronym
a turning point (war, bankruptcy, technology shift)
a cultural adoption moment with named figures
steps: Open with the quirk — "X wasn't meant to be Y, but that's what makes it iconic."
Drop the origin in one sentence (year, person).
Reveal the name's etymology.
Name the turning point.
Close on the cultural moment that cemented it.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Five beats is the right size — under that feels thin, over feels lectured. Dan's version can drop on-camera entirely: timeline visual + voice-over works.
recipe.dan_version: "Helvetica wasn't meant to be a UI typeface, but that's what makes it inescapable." Origin: 1957, Max Miedinger, Switzerland. Name: "Helvetia" = Latin for Switzerland. Turning point: 1984 Macintosh. Cultural moment: every iOS button until 2015.

dan_versions.design: "Helvetica wasn't meant to be a UI typeface, but that's what makes it inescapable." Origin: 1957, Max Miedinger, Switzerland. Name: "Helvetia" = Latin for Switzerland. Turning point: 1984 Macintosh. Cultural moment: every iOS button until 2015.

recipe.what_happens: Etymology + cultural-arc explainer. Opens with the anomaly (worn backwards), then origin (1938 Jacques Sprayrigan importing Basque berets), then name acronym (K-ANG-OL = silk/Angora/wool), then WWII contract, then post-war 504 cap (Beatles, Princess Margaret), then 80s NYC hip-hop adoption (LL Cool J, Run DMC). Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: "why is this iconic thing the way it is" hooks immediately. Acronym reveal ("K stands for silk") is a memorable detail viewers will repeat.
recipe.title: @drewjoiner — Etymology + cultural-arc explainer

recipes.caption: September’s senses.
recipes.hook: Okay guys, it's September which means nobody asked for it but I'm going to share with you my five circles.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: it's a series with a fixed format, so the audience knows what they're getting. The self-aware "nobody asked" disarms — it's not pretending the post is necessary. Five categories give specific recommendations across taste/lifestyle, which is what the audience is actually there for.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Excellent fit as a recurring monthly format. Five categories per month is enough to write but not enough to drift. The self-aware opener is in Dan's voice. Categories could be: design (one piece of work seen), tool (one new tool tried), book/reference, family/life moment, build progress.

recipe.name: Monthly five-category recap (series)
recipe.concept: A recurring monthly post with a fixed five-category structure. One specific recommendation per category. Self-aware opener that admits the post is unrequested.
ingredients: five fixed categories
one specific recommendation per category (named, not vague)
a self-aware opener
a defined cadence (first of every month)
a recognisable visual setup for the series
steps: Open with the month + self-aware framing.
Walk each category in the same order every month.
Make every recommendation specific — name the book, the dish, the show.
Keep each category to one sentence.
Close with a soft line, not a sign-off CTA.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Dan-specific categories should sit in his actual practice. Suggest: design seen, tool tried, reference book, family moment, build progress. Stick to the same five for at least 6 months — series effects compound.
recipe.dan_version: "Okay, it's May. Nobody asked for it but here's the monthly five." Design seen: [specific work, named]. Tool tried: [specific tool]. Reference: [book + page]. Family moment: [specific Rowie thing]. Build progress: [Lia / Voxquote / NOBONK update].

dan_versions.design: "Okay, it's May. Nobody asked for it but here's the monthly five." Design seen: [specific work, named]. Tool tried: [specific tool]. Reference: [book + page]. Family moment: [specific Rowie thing]. Build progress: [Lia / Voxquote / NOBONK update].

recipe.what_happens: Monthly five-senses recap. Walks taste → sight → smell → touch → hearing, one specific recommendation per sense. "Five circles" is a transcript error for "five senses." Self-aware framing: "nobody asked for it." Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: it's a series with a fixed format, so the audience knows what they're getting. The self-aware "nobody asked" disarms — it's not pretending the post is necessary. Five categories give specific recommendations across taste/lifestyle, which is what the audience is actually there for.
recipe.title: @jeremiahbrent — Monthly five-category recap (series)

recipes.creator: ryan_holiday
recipes.caption: Cosigning on this recommendation with Daniel Radcliffe because it is ...
recipes.hook: Daniel Radcliffe, Harry Potter has been raving about this book. I just want to co-sign on this.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: co-signing a celebrity recommendation gives the post two credibility layers — Ryan's and Radcliffe's. The tie-in to current events (Bad Bunny Super Bowl) makes it timely. Personal anecdote (NBC commission) makes it feel like real recommendation, not a plug.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Moderate fit. The pattern — "X recommended Y, I'm cosigning, here's why it matters now" — works for design references. Dan could cosign a designer's reading list, a founder's framework, an artist's process.

recipe.name: Co-sign with current-events tie-in
recipe.concept: Cosign someone else's recommendation. Tie it to a current event or your own work to make it timely. Two credibility layers carry the post.
ingredients: someone else's existing recommendation
a current event or recent work it connects to
a brief personal anecdote
the actual recommendation (book, tool, framework)
minimal sell
steps: Name who recommended it.
State the recommendation.
Tie it to a current event or your own work.
Add the personal anecdote.
Don't sell — just cosign.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Only cosign things Dan has actually engaged with. Fake cosigns are obvious.
recipe.dan_version: "Massimo Vignelli's Canon has been recommended by every designer I respect this year. I want to cosign. The 'noise to signal' chapter mapped exactly onto a Whiting decision I made last month."

dan_versions.design: "Massimo Vignelli's Canon has been recommended by every designer I respect this year. I want to cosign. The 'noise to signal' chapter mapped exactly onto a Whiting decision I made last month."

recipe.what_happens: Celebrity co-sign book recommendation. Ryan references Daniel Radcliffe's earlier recommendation of a book about Puerto Rico, then ties it into Bad Bunny's Super Bowl performance and his own NBC work. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: co-signing a celebrity recommendation gives the post two credibility layers — Ryan's and Radcliffe's. The tie-in to current events (Bad Bunny Super Bowl) makes it timely. Personal anecdote (NBC commission) makes it feel like real recommendation, not a plug.
recipe.title: @ryan_holiday — Co-sign with current-events tie-in

recipes.creator: sahilbloom
recipes.caption: The truth about finding "The One"... #selfhelp #viraltiktok #sahilblo...
recipes.hook: I don't think you find an ideal partner.
recipes.why_it_worked:
- Strong reversal of common language: `find` → `create`.
- Relationship content has broad emotional reach.
- The claim feels mature and useful, not just motivational.
- Shareable because it gives people a better phrase for something they already half-believe.
recipes.fit_for_dan:
“I don’t think you find your taste. I think you build it.”

Use around design judgement, creative practice, or fatherhood. Strong fit if grounded in an actual scene: looking at references, choosing materials, iterating a layout, showing up tired but making one better decision.

recipe.name: The truth about finding “The One”

Explain why the common verb is passive or misleading.
Define the thing as repeated small decisions.
End with the warning: you can have the right thing and still ruin it by not showing up.

recipes.creator: jeremiahbrent
recipes.caption: 10/10 would marry again ;)⁠ ⁠ Happy anniversary, @Nate Berkus Lifestyle
recipes.hook: 10/10 would marry again
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: relationship/anniversary content has very broad reach. The light-touch caption ("10/10 would marry again") is the post — it's plain and self-aware. Background dialogue makes the partnership feel ongoing rather than staged.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Moderate fit. Anniversary content is personal and Dan doesn't share Hannah on TikTok by default. The transferable insight is using a casual partnership-in-action background while the caption does the actual statement.

recipe.name: Caption-led relationship moment
recipe.concept: A relationship/anniversary post that lets the visuals (and incidental background dialogue) carry the partnership texture while the caption makes the statement plainly.
ingredients: a real moment with a partner (not posed)
incidental dialogue audible in the background
a plain, slightly self-aware caption
no on-camera monologue
date-stamped framing (anniversary, milestone)
steps: Capture a moment that's actually happening (decluttering, cooking, walking).
Don't direct the moment.
Write a caption that's plain, slightly funny, low-key.
Skip a voice-over.
Tag the partner if you both want it public.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Only run this format if Dan and Hannah agree on the level of visibility. The structure is good — the question is whether the family layer of Dan's TikTok includes this kind of content.
recipe.dan_version: If/when this format is wanted: a Sunday morning with Rowie and Hannah, no monologue, background dialogue. Caption: "Bath time, third year in." Nothing performative.

dan_versions.design: If/when this format is wanted: a Sunday morning with Rowie and Hannah, no monologue, background dialogue. Caption: "Bath time, third year in." Nothing performative.

recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: relationship/anniversary content has very broad reach. The light-touch caption ("10/10 would marry again") is the post — it's plain and self-aware. Background dialogue makes the partnership feel ongoing rather than staged.

recipes.creator: prestonkonrad
recipes.caption: This Victoria Beckham bronze water tint is next level
recipes.hook: This might be one of my favorite kind of bronzing products I ever have used.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: enthusiastic but specific product review. Names the exact product, shows two application methods, calls out a downside (staining on hands). Specifics build trust over hype.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Moderate fit. The pattern — enthusiastic but specific review with one downside — translates to design tool reviews. Lean into specificity over enthusiasm.

recipe.name: Specific-with-one-downside product review
recipe.concept: Enthusiastic product review that earns trust by naming exact specs, demonstrating use, and calling out at least one downside.
ingredients: a real product being recommended
the exact name and spec
a demonstration of use
at least one honest downside
no sponsorship (or fully disclosed)
steps: Open with the enthusiastic but specific claim.
Name the exact product.
Demonstrate use in one motion.
Call out one downside.
Close on a specific use-case.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Downside has to be real. Performed honesty is worse than enthusiasm.
recipe.dan_version: "Cursor's $20/mo plan is the best AI dev environment I've used. Specifically the Composer mode. The downside: it's hard to undo a multi-file change cleanly. But the speed makes up for it. Best for projects with under 50 files."

dan_versions.design: "Cursor's $20/mo plan is the best AI dev environment I've used. Specifically the Composer mode. The downside: it's hard to undo a multi-file change cleanly. But the speed makes up for it. Best for projects with under 50 files."

recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: enthusiastic but specific product review. Names the exact product, shows two application methods, calls out a downside (staining on hands). Specifics build trust over hype.
recipe.title: @prestonkonrad — Specific-with-one-downside product review

recipes.creator: eyeswoon
recipes.caption: How I pit an olive for prettier plating 🫒 You may have noticed a cert...
recipes.hook: So easy nobody loves a kitchen hack more than me
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: tip + demonstration + visible result is the canonical kitchen-TikTok shape. What lifts this from generic hack content is the closing line — the hack isn't justified by speed or convenience, it's justified by aesthetics. That's the eyeswoon position — taste, not utility.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong structural fit. The pattern translates directly: "here's a workflow shortcut I use" + demonstrate + close on the aesthetic or quality reason, not the time saved. Dan's version should anchor the close on taste/quality, not productivity.

recipe.name: Hack-with-aesthetic-justification
recipe.concept: Demonstrate a workflow shortcut, but close on the aesthetic or quality reason it works — not the time saved.
ingredients: a specific repeated action (kitchen, design, code)
a non-obvious technique
one clear demonstration
a payoff frame that shows the result
a closing line that names taste, not speed
steps: State the hack plainly: "Here's how I X."
Show the technique in one continuous motion.
Repeat it once to confirm.
Cut to the result.
Close on the aesthetic or quality reason — never "saves time."
recipe.adaptation_notes: The discipline is in the close. Most hack content closes on convenience. Closing on taste is the differentiator.
recipe.dan_version: "Here's how I sketch a logo without going to Pinterest first." Show the technique. Show the result. Close: "The hand-drawn version always has more personality than the moodboard pull."

dan_versions.design: "Here's how I sketch a logo without going to Pinterest first." Show the technique. Show the result. Close: "The hand-drawn version always has more personality than the moodboard pull."

recipe.what_happens: Kitchen hack demonstration: pitting olives by smashing them with the side of a knife. The payoff is aesthetic, not just functional: "beautiful tattered edges which look better when you're plating." Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: tip + demonstration + visible result is the canonical kitchen-TikTok shape. What lifts this from generic hack content is the closing line — the hack isn't justified by speed or convenience, it's justified by aesthetics. That's the eyeswoon position — taste, not utility.
recipe.title: @eyeswoon — Hack-with-aesthetic-justification

One sentence per category.

recipes.creator: prestonkonrad
recipes.caption: Met gala secrets from the carpet…do u want more? What do u wanna know?
recipes.hook: So the Met Gala is tomorrow and as someone who's been on the carpet for a few years, let's get into it.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: insider POV of an event everyone watches from outside is high-leverage content. Specifics — positions, times, who shares the step — earn the credibility. Caption invites more questions, extending the format into a series.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Moderate fit. The pattern — "insider POV of a thing people see from outside" — translates to design/AI events Dan attends or works on. Endava client kickoff from a senior designer's seat. The first hour of a brand workshop. Conference green rooms.

recipe.name: Insider POV of a public event
recipe.concept: Stand inside an event the audience sees from outside. Walk specific details — positions, times, who's where — that only the inside reveals.
ingredients: a public event you have insider access to
specific positional details
specific times and orders
named co-workers (camera, producer, etc.)
an invitation to ask more in caption
steps: Open with the event + your access role.
Name a specific position or detail.
Walk the schedule.
Show what's shared (a step, a room, a green room).
Invite questions for more posts.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Access has to be real. Dan can do this for Endava client meetings, design jams, brand workshops — any event where the audience-facing version doesn't match the insider view.
recipe.dan_version: "So we're presenting the new design system to the exec team tomorrow. As someone who's done this for a few clients, let's get into what actually happens." Walk the prep: the order of the deck, who's in the room, what gets cut last minute.

dan_versions.design: "So we're presenting the new design system to the exec team tomorrow. As someone who's done this for a few clients, let's get into what actually happens." Walk the prep: the order of the deck, who's in the room, what gets cut last minute.

recipe.what_happens: Insider POV of working the Met Gala carpet. Names the position ("third in this stall"), the order (ET, E, US), the schedule (3pm to midnight), the shared step setup with cameraman/producer/reporter. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: insider POV of an event everyone watches from outside is high-leverage content. Specifics — positions, times, who shares the step — earn the credibility. Caption invites more questions, extending the format into a series.
recipe.title: @prestonkonrad — Insider POV of a public event

recipes.creator: ryan_holiday
recipes.caption: I was on Morning Joe last week to talk about my favorite things, book...
recipes.hook: I was at my son's lacrosse game the other day, and I saw one of the other parents reading the Count of Monte Cristo.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: anecdote-led recommendations beat list-style "3 books you need to read". The lacrosse-game framing is specific and human. The reveal — Dumas's fiction has real historical roots — gives the audience a hook for sharing the post.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit for Dan's reading life. The pattern — small personal anecdote → book name → one surprising fact about why it matters — works for design and AI reading lists. Run it the way Dan would actually share a book with a friend.

recipe.name: Anecdote-led book recommendation with reveal
recipe.concept: Open with a small specific anecdote that triggered the recommendation. Name the book. Reveal one surprising fact that makes the book matter.
ingredients: a small specific personal anecdote
the book's name and author
one surprising fact
no list-style framing
no CTA
steps: Open with the anecdote.
Name the book that came up.
Tell the surprising fact.
Don't sell it — let the fact carry it.
Close without a CTA.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Anecdote must be real. Faked context kills the format.
recipe.dan_version: "I was helping a founder with their brand last week and they asked what the difference between 'system' and 'guideline' actually is. So I sent them Massimo Vignelli's Canon. What people don't realise is Vignelli wrote it as a free PDF in 2010 — it's the most influential design book of the last 20 years and it's literally free."

dan_versions.design: "I was helping a founder with their brand last week and they asked what the difference between 'system' and 'guideline' actually is. So I sent them Massimo Vignelli's Canon. What people don't realise is Vignelli wrote it as a free PDF in 2010 — it's the most influential design book of the last 20 years and it's literally free."

recipe.what_happens: Anecdote-led book recommendation. Lacrosse-game parent reading Dumas. Ryan asks if they know about The Black Count — book about Dumas's father, a Black general in Napoleon's army, born a slave in Haiti. Recommendation lands via the surprise reveal that Dumas's fiction was rooted in his father's real life. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: anecdote-led recommendations beat list-style "3 books you need to read". The lacrosse-game framing is specific and human. The reveal — Dumas's fiction has real historical roots — gives the audience a hook for sharing the post.
recipe.title: @ryan_holiday — Anecdote-led book recommendation with reveal

recipes.creator: prestonkonrad
recipes.caption: What do u think of this new ikea collection??
recipes.hook: IKEA has just launched their new PS collection. Design lovers, you will be obsessed.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: archive-led brand reviews satisfy both new viewers (here's a thing existing) and old viewers (here's the lineage). The 1995 origin gives the new collection credibility. Specific pieces (bent steel lamps) give the audience something to actually want.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit. The pattern — "here's what just launched, here's the historical context, here are the specific pieces" — works for design system releases, AI tool launches, type foundry drops. The historical context is the differentiator.

recipe.name: Brand-archive review with lineage
recipe.concept: Review a new release by anchoring it to the brand's archive. Origin year, original intent, what's changed, what's new. Pick 2-3 specific items.
ingredients: a new release from a brand with history
the origin year of the line
the original intent of the line
2-3 specific new items
an opinion on whether it's worth attention
steps: Hook: "X just dropped. Here's why it matters."
Anchor to the origin year and intent.
Note what's the same/changed.
Walk 2-3 specific items.
Close on an opinion.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Don't run if you don't actually know the brand's archive. Faking historical context is the fastest way to lose design audiences.
recipe.dan_version: "Stripe just shipped a new docs design. Here's why it matters. The first Stripe docs in 2011 set the template every modern docs site copies. This refresh kept the type hierarchy but moved the code samples — that's the change worth noting. Specifically these three pages…"

dan_versions.design: "Stripe just shipped a new docs design. Here's why it matters. The first Stripe docs in 2011 set the template every modern docs site copies. This refresh kept the type hierarchy but moved the code samples — that's the change worth noting. Specifically these three pages…"

recipe.what_happens: Brand-archive review of IKEA PS collection. Walks history (1995 first PS = Post-Script, focus on playful affordable design), names this as the 10th collection, then highlights specific pieces (bent steel lamps with swivel heads). Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: archive-led brand reviews satisfy both new viewers (here's a thing existing) and old viewers (here's the lineage). The 1995 origin gives the new collection credibility. Specific pieces (bent steel lamps) give the audience something to actually want.
recipe.title: @prestonkonrad — Brand-archive review with lineage

the colour's actual name
the material's specific type

a slow camera pace

Name the brand and the object.
Use the actual colour name.
Identify the material specifically.
Call out one signature feature.

recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: reverent unboxing with material-name specificity reads as connoisseurship, not consumerism. Each named detail (colour name, signature feature, lining type) is the work. Audience watches because the host actually cares.

recipes.creator: nicolasfairford
recipes.caption: How to take care of your candles
recipes.hook: Candles actually have a memory. So the first time that you light it is very, very important.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: "X has a memory" is a sticky hook because it personifies an object viewers don't think about. The specific 4-hour rule is actionable. Educational mini-tutorials about everyday objects perform when the detail is genuinely useful and counterintuitive.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit. The pattern — "this everyday thing has a counterintuitive rule" — works for design tools, AI prompts, type setting. "Your design system has a memory. The first time you name a token sets the convention for every future one."

recipe.name: Counterintuitive small-detail tutorial
recipe.concept: Take a familiar everyday object or tool. Reveal one counterintuitive rule about how to use it correctly. Make the rule actionable.
ingredients: a familiar object/tool the audience uses
one counterintuitive rule
a specific number or threshold
a clear reason why the rule exists
a visible consequence of breaking it
steps: Open with the surprising claim: "X has a memory."
State the rule.
Show the specific threshold (4 hours, 80%, etc.).
Explain why.
Show what happens if you don't.
recipe.adaptation_notes: The rule must be real and verifiable. Made-up rules collapse fast under design-audience scrutiny.
recipe.dan_version: "Your design system has a memory. The first time you name a primary colour token (`primary` vs `brand-primary` vs `color-primary-500`) sets the convention for every future token. If you change it later, you'll be refactoring for months. Pick once. Pick carefully."

dan_versions.design: "Your design system has a memory. The first time you name a primary colour token (`primary` vs `brand-primary` vs `color-primary-500`) sets the convention for every future token. If you change it later, you'll be refactoring for months. Pick once. Pick carefully."

recipe.what_happens: Counterintuitive small-detail tutorial. Candle care: first burn must last ~4 hours so wax melts evenly across the whole surface — otherwise the candle tunnels. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: "X has a memory" is a sticky hook because it personifies an object viewers don't think about. The specific 4-hour rule is actionable. Educational mini-tutorials about everyday objects perform when the detail is genuinely useful and counterintuitive.
recipe.title: @nicolasfairford — Counterintuitive small-detail tutorial

recipes.caption: 30 Days of Elegance - Day Six
recipes.hook: Hello and welcome back to 30 days of elegance where I give you a few tips each day so that by the end of January you can become a truly elegant person.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: 30-day series gives the audience a built-in reason to return daily. Each episode is small enough to be one tip. Numbered episodes (Day 6) signal scarcity and progress.

recipe.name: Numbered-day series with one tip per episode
recipe.concept: A 21- or 30-day series where each post is one specific actionable tip, numbered (Day N). The series creates daily return habit.
ingredients: a clear theme that supports 21-30 tips
a numbered counter (Day N of 30)
one tip per episode
60-90 second runtime
a recognisable opening line
steps: Plan all 30 topics before posting Day 1.
Standardise the opening line.
One tip per post.
Number the day in the caption + on screen.
Don't skip days.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Plan all 30 before starting. Format collapses if you run out of tips mid-series.
recipe.dan_version: "30 Days of Design Judgement. Day 1: when in doubt, kill the slide." Series of one-tip posts running through July. Pre-plan all 30.

recipes.creator: brad_leone
recipes.caption: The more you know…
recipes.hook: Anyone you know named Cooper, that's what they did for a living.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: name-origin reveals are share-fuel. Audience thinks about people they know with those surnames. The cross-language list (German, English) extends the bit. Crude line ("put a cock on it") gives it Brad's signature character.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Moderate fit. The pattern — etymology list across familiar names/words — translates to design and AI terminology. "Anyone you've ever used 'serif' on, here's why it's called that." Origin of typography terms, brand naming patterns, AI jargon.

recipe.name: Etymology list across a familiar set
recipe.concept: Pick a familiar set (surnames, type terms, brand names). List 5-7 etymologies in rapid sequence. Audience invests because they recognise the items.
ingredients: a familiar set with 5+ items
the origin/meaning of each
rapid pacing
one anchor that lands the bit (a joke, a punchline)
cross-category extension if possible
steps: Open with one example.
Add 3-4 more in sequence.
Drop a punchline mid-list.
Extend across category (other language, other field).
Close on the most absurd one.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Real etymologies. The format dies if facts are wrong.
recipe.dan_version: "Anyone you've used the word 'serif' on, here's where it comes from. Serif = the small line at the end of a stroke. Greek, possibly. Sans-serif = without serif. Roman. Bauhaus. Helvetica is Latin for 'Swiss'. Garamond is just a French guy's name. Bodoni is just an Italian guy's name. Type history is mostly just dead people's last names."

dan_versions.design: "Anyone you've used the word 'serif' on, here's where it comes from. Serif = the small line at the end of a stroke. Greek, possibly. Sans-serif = without serif. Roman. Bauhaus. Helvetica is Latin for 'Swiss'. Garamond is just a French guy's name. Bodoni is just an Italian guy's name. Type history is mostly just dead people's last names."

recipe.what_happens: Occupational surname etymology. Cooper = barrel maker, Sawyer = mill worker, Miller = miller, Kellogg = killed hogs, Hancock/Hitchcock = had a cock(?), Foss/Fossbender = barrel makers in German, Baker = baked. Listed across English and German. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: name-origin reveals are share-fuel. Audience thinks about people they know with those surnames. The cross-language list (German, English) extends the bit. Crude line ("put a cock on it") gives it Brad's signature character.
recipe.title: @brad_leone — Etymology list across a familiar set

recipes.creator: deeplymadlymodern
recipes.caption: Replying to @Blank Face naur babe, you can do it! Promise! #affordabl...
recipes.hook: Let's say that you can afford $50 a month to decorate. This is a little more proof that it's not your budget, but it's your imagination.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: the budget constraint + itemised receipts is one of the most replicable formats on TikTok. The audience can do the maths along with the host. The thesis ("not your budget, your imagination") is a recurring frame across multiple posts.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit translated. The pattern — "here's a hard constraint, here's how to spend it well" — works for AI tooling budgets, brand budgets, design tool stacks. "$50 a month on design tools. Here's the stack."

recipe.name: Hard-budget itemised demonstration
recipe.concept: Set a specific hard budget. Walk through itemised purchases with prices visible on screen. Sum at the end. Frame as imagination over budget.
ingredients: a specific dollar constraint
4-7 itemised purchases
each price shown on screen
a running or final total
a thesis line about budget vs imagination
steps: State the budget upfront.
Walk each item with its price.
Keep the running total visible.
Sum at the end.
Land the thesis.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Real prices. Sales links matter — the format trades on actual replicability.
recipe.dan_version: "$50/month on AI tooling. Here's the stack that actually ships work. Claude Pro $20. Cursor $20. Linear free tier. Figma free seat. Vercel hobby tier. Total: $40 with $10 left for the occasional one-off. Not your budget. Your taste."

dan_versions.design: "$50/month on AI tooling. Here's the stack that actually ships work. Claude Pro $20. Cursor $20. Linear free tier. Figma free seat. Vercel hobby tier. Total: $40 with $10 left for the occasional one-off. Not your budget. Your taste."

recipe.what_happens: Budget-constraint decorating series. $50 monthly budget. Walks specific items: $4.99 rug from At Home, $8.99/$6.99 items, $20 baskets, $5 vase (from $10). Totals $46. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.

Tag the real client or project for credibility.
Keep it short — don't overstay the joke.

recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit. The pattern — "here's what it actually looked like when I committed" — works for Dan's clothing brand era, the freelance start, the AI-tooling pivot. Specific gritty detail beats motivational framing.

recipe.name: Burn-the-boats origin story
recipe.concept: Tell the specific moment you committed past the point of safe return. Real gritty details. No motivational framing.
ingredients: a specific decision point
the unsafe choice you made
gritty material details (no fridge, no money, one person showed up)
no motivational language
the result implied, not stated
steps: Open with the year and the decision.
Name the unsafe move.
List 3-4 gritty details that prove the commitment.
Show the first-day result.
Don't moralise — let it stand.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Gritty details are the work. "I went all-in" is worthless; "I had a second phone number and was sitting in the bed of my truck" is the post.
recipe.dan_version: "Year two of the clothing brand, I had $0 in the account and pre-orders open. I shipped from my bedroom for six months. recipes.hook: When they say a woman has to slow down, become smaller, take up less space, calm down — no, absolutely not.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: a sharp standalone declaration with a clear position works as a tone-setter post. The audience knows what the creator stands for in 15 seconds. Manifesto posts perform as bookmarks for fans, not conversion.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Moderate fit. The pattern — a single sharp position stated plainly — works for Dan on specific design or AI takes. The key is having a position worth holding, not performing one.

recipe.name: Manifesto declaration
recipe.concept: A short standalone statement of position. No setup, no story, no example. The position is the whole post.
ingredients: a clearly held position
no setup
rhetorical questions if any
direct delivery
no resolution
steps: Open with the position.
State it twice if needed.
Close on a rhetorical question.
No softening.
Cut.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Only run if the position is genuinely held and would hold under pushback. Performed manifestos read worst of all.
recipe.dan_version: 'When clients say the AI did the work, no. The brief was the work. The decisions were the work. The selection of which 1 of 200 outputs to keep was the work. The AI typed. Get over yourselves.'

dan_versions.design: 'When clients say the AI did the work, no. The brief was the work. The decisions were the work. The selection of which 1 of 200 outputs to keep was the work. The AI typed. Get over yourselves.'

no CTA
an emotional anchor (mortality, change, family)

recipe.name: Inhabit-the-critic short defense
recipe.concept: Voice the recurring criticism in the critic's voice. Don't respond. Let the audience hear how the criticism sounds out loud.
ingredients: a recurring criticism
the critic's voice (faux/imitated)
no rebuttal
very short runtime
a caption that contradicts the criticism
steps: Imitate the critic in one line.
Don't respond.
Caption: the contradiction.
Cut.
No follow-up needed.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Don't run this for criticisms Dan can't take. The format reads as confident if the position is held, defensive if the position is shaky.

recipe.name: Reframe-the-criticism rant
recipe.concept: Take the recurring criticism. Reframe it as the critic's actual problem. Not defense, redirection.
ingredients: a recurring criticism with a clear shape
the underlying fear/deficiency you're naming
a sharp one-liner that lands the reframe
no apology
no follow-up needed
steps: Open with 'You know what I hate?'
State the criticism.
Name what it actually reveals about the critic.
Land the one-liner.
Cut.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Use rarely. The format only works in isolation; multiple reframe rants in a row read as bitterness.
recipe.dan_version: 'You know what I hate? Designers calling AI-assisted work "cheating". Cheating what? Your subjective preference for the slow version? Process isn't sacred. Outcomes are.'

dan_versions.design: 'You know what I hate? Designers calling AI-assisted work "cheating". Cheating what? Your subjective preference for the slow version? Process isn't sacred. Outcomes are.'

recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: rant format with a specific recurring criticism, reframed as the critic's deficiency. The reframe ('it's about commitment, they're afraid of it') turns defense into offense. Lands as identity-validation for fans.

recipes.creator: sahilbloom
recipes.caption: This is the most attractive trait... #selfhelp #viraltiktok #sahilbloom
recipes.hook: The most attractive trait isn't looks wealth or status, it's energy.
recipes.why_it_worked:
- Very short and quotable.
- Uses a familiar list: looks, wealth, status.
- Offers a more controllable alternative: energy.
- Positive and shareable without requiring much context.
recipes.fit_for_dan:
“The most underrated design skill isn’t taste, tools, or speed. It’s attention.”

Could also become: “The most attractive thing in a room isn’t the furniture. It’s care.” Good for taste/interiors/content, but keep it specific so it doesn’t become quote-card mush.

Tags: `short-aphorism`, `status-reversal`, `energy`, `quote-card-risk`, `simple-recipe`

---

## Pattern readout

Sahil’s recent winners are not complex edits. They win through compressed self-help structures:

1. **Contrarian reframe:** “You don’t find X, you create Y.”
2. **Research-backed warning:** small behaviour has hidden relational cost.
3. **Permission frame:** you are not responsible for others’ discomfort.
4. **Named concept:** there’s a term for why you feel stuck.
5. **Status reversal:** the real trait isn’t obvious status, it’s something controllable.

## What this means for Dan

The useful part is the structure, not the tone. Directly copying Sahil would be too motivational and generic for Dan. The transferable move is:

- open with a clean reframe
- attach it to a real life/work scene
- make the insight specific to design, taste, AI, fatherhood, or building
- end with a small practical action, not a motivational command

## Safer next step

Run this same top-five test on a creator closer to Dan’s desired tone, like `@matt_davella`, `@BeauMiles`, or `@colinkingstudio`. Sahil is useful for structures, but he is high-cringe-risk if treated as a voice model.

recipe.name: This is the most attractive trait
recipe.concept: Short aphoristic post. It ranks expected status markers against a softer trait, then turns that trait into a social principle.
steps: “The most [desired quality] trait isn’t [obvious 1], [obvious 2], or [obvious 3]. It’s [deeper trait].”
Explain how the deeper trait changes how people experience you.
End with a memorable line.
recipe.adaptation_notes:
“The most underrated design skill isn’t taste, tools, or speed. It’s attention.”

Could also become: “The most attractive thing in a room isn’t the furniture. It’s care.” Good for taste/interiors/content, but keep it specific so it doesn’t become quote-card mush.
