recipes.creator: matt_davella
recipes.caption: 2 day rule #mattdavella #fyp
recipes.hook: Our brains are really good at thinking, but they are terrible at remembering.
recipes.why_it_worked: Likely: calm, well-structured voice-over essays with named principles travel further than punchy hot takes for the right audience. The example (forgetting a brilliant idea overnight) is universally felt. The framing — "rules as cognitive prosthesis" — is fresh enough to feel insightful.
recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit. Same register as Dan's actual writing voice. Voice-over essays work for: how design systems work, why brand consistency compounds, what AI tools actually change about creative practice.

recipe.name: Voice-over essay with named principle
recipe.concept: Calm voice-over walking a single named principle. Open with a counter-intuitive observation, give one universal example, build toward the larger claim.
ingredients: a named principle worth naming
a counter-intuitive opening observation
one universal everyday example
a build toward a larger claim
calm visual b-roll, not jumpy
steps: Open with the counter-intuitive observation in plain language.
Give the universal example fast.
Name the principle.
Build the claim in 2-3 sentences.
Leave the conclusion implied, not stated.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Dan's voice is already in this register. The work is in picking principles that haven't been said 50 times. "Design systems are cognitive prosthesis for brands" is more interesting than "design systems save time."
recipe.dan_version: "AI tools are really good at writing, but they're terrible at deciding. That's why an AI-built brand still needs a designer — to decide which of the 50 generated options is actually the one. The job isn't generation anymore. It's selection."

dan_versions.design: "AI tools are really good at writing, but they're terrible at deciding. That's why an AI-built brand still needs a designer — to decide which of the 50 generated options is actually the one. The job isn't generation anymore. It's selection."

recipe.what_happens: Voice-over essay on why rules and systems work. Opens with the cognition split (thinking vs. remembering). Uses the lost-business-idea-before-bed example. Builds toward biological wiring for distraction. Visual unverified — transcript-based analysis only.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Likely: calm, well-structured voice-over essays with named principles travel further than punchy hot takes for the right audience. The example (forgetting a brilliant idea overnight) is universally felt. The framing — "rules as cognitive prosthesis" — is fresh enough to feel insightful.
recipe.title: @matt_davella — Voice-over essay with named principle

recipes.creator: matt_davella

recipes.fit_for_dan: Strong fit. The pattern — "there's a name for this thing you feel" + named source — works for design and creative work. Dan can cite work he actually reads (Charles Eames, Massimo Vignelli, Bret Victor, specific essays).

recipe.name: Named-source essay with vocabulary gift
recipe.concept: Voice-over essay built around a named work and a named concept from it. The viewer leaves with a new word for something they already felt.
ingredients: a named book or essay
a named concept from it
a list of how that concept shows up in everyday life
a direct quote if available
a single takeaway, implicit
steps: Open with a claim that demands a why.
Cite the named work in one sentence.
Define the named concept.
List 3-4 ways it shows up.
Close on the concept's stakes.
recipe.adaptation_notes: Cite work Dan actually reads. The format dies if the reference is fake.
recipe.dan_version: "There's a reason the best designers I know all have a 'no Pinterest until I've sketched' rule. Charles Eames called it 'the discipline of constraints' — limit your inputs early, even when you don't have to, because constraints generate options the open canvas never will."

recipes.creator: bilalrehmanstudio
recipes.caption: Part 11 | a little look at the color palette we’ve decided on for thi...
recipes.hook:
So the rug's got delivered today and I thought this was the perfect time to show you guys the color palette
That we chose for all of the rugs throughout the house a lot of the rugs that we did are super neutral
But we did throw in a pop of color here and there where we needed to because we can't paint the space after all
So we have this really insane green rug that I'm absolutely obsessed with ivory rug
It is so beautiful and we have this going into a couple of different spaces
This chocolate brown is probably one of my favorite ones
I actually have this exact same rug in my bedroom at home
And I'm obsessed with it and so we chose to pull the same rug into this project and I'm so glad that we did
Recently, I've been on such a huge kick of yellow and orange so obsessed with these colors
I felt they add so much warmth into the room
Especially like right now the way the sun is hitting this and it's bouncing the orange light into the space
Just it's so magical.

recipe.concept: Open with a sharp quote or observation, name the quiet drift, then make it concrete through Dan's world: design taste, building, fatherhood, or constrained creative time.
ingredients: one quote or blunt observation about time, taste, or creative drift
a specific Dan-life scene, not abstract motivation
a visible project, object, screen, notebook, ride, or family-time constraint
a restrained final line that points to action without guru energy
steps: Open with the line: 'Some people stop making things long before they stop working' or a better Dan-native equivalent.
Show a concrete scene: laptop at night, design file, messy notes, kid monitor, gym bag, morning light, or project materials.
Name the tension: it is easy to optimise, scroll, or plan instead of actually making the thing.
Use 2-3 quick 'why not' questions, but keep them dry and specific rather than motivational.
End with a small action: open the file, sketch the first frame, publish the rough thought, record the note before bath time.
recipe.adaptation_notes: The original is broad motivational urgency. Dan's version should make the same structure quieter, more specific, and more credible through actual design/family/building texture.
recipe.why_it_worked_rich: Cannot make a causal claim from transcript/visible metrics alone. Treat high visible views as a selection signal, then manually review the post for actual pattern extraction.
recipe.title: Quote → drift diagnosis → Dan-specific wake-up from @dan..robson
